Seasonal Vector Tiles — Animation & Keyframe Evaluation

A close-up audit of all 80 hand-drawn, all-vector animated board tiles (src/textures/seasonal/**): their four per-season keyframes, the two-tier "WC3-style" idle, and the forward season→season transitions — rendered faithfully on the real board disc and reviewed tile-by-tile.

Evaluation · 2026-06-23 · scope: the vector seasonal pipeline (NOT the PixelLab pixel-art route) · 80 tiles across 14 board families

80 tiles 14 families 50 tiles ≥ 4.0/5 6 P0 bugs 17 tiles have no rare idle close-up + source review

Overview

The recent "bold & fun" rollout set out to make the seasonal tiles read obviously across seasons and feel alive with a two-tier, WarCraft-III-style idle (a calm common beat plus a rare bold action), all stitched with seamless season→season morphs. For the land creatures and most produce, it lands beautifully. The machinery is genuinely excellent and the character animation is the high point of the whole art set.

TL;DR. The core animation system is provably solid — seamless idles and exact transition endpoints, measured to the pixel. 62% of tiles (50/80) score ≥ 4.0. But three things hold the set back from uniform quality: (1) the two-tier WC3 idle was never rolled out to fish (8) or minerals/coin/special (9) — 17 tiles have no rare action at all; (2) a cluster of winter keyframes "white out" the subject instead of frosting it; and (3) 6 locatable correctness bugs, one of which (the giant pearl) renders the subject unrecognizable.

How the set scores

Overall-score distribution (80 tiles)

Average by biome family

*grass/flower combined; spiky/pansy/water-lily are 5.0, grass/heather/clover drag it down.

The shape of the result is unusually clean: the weakness is concentrated by biome, not scattered. Land animals and produce are strong-to-exemplary; the soft spots are almost entirely fish, minerals, the special pearl, and a handful of named plant tiles. That makes the fixes very targetable.

Direct answers to the brief

Do they look interesting & lively enough?

Land creatures & produce: yes — genuinely lively, with real character. Fish & minerals: not yet — they animate with a single gentle continuous loop and no bold beat, so they feel sleepy next to the birds. The liveliness gap is concentrated in those 17 tiles.

Especially the WC3 special idles?

Excellent where they exist (birds, herd, cattle, mounts): bespoke, on-theme rare actions — a rooster's crow, a pheasant's flush, a goat's head-butt — measured at 1.5–4× the common-beat energy and perfectly seamless. But absent on fish & minerals, and weak on a few land tiles (wild-goose, boar, grass). See the gap.

Per-season keyframes — good, or change/enhance?

Mostly good & boldly differentiated for plants/animals. Change needed for the winter "white-out" tiles (carrot, broccoli, squash, pear, oak, birch, willow, manna), the flat mineral seasons (all 7 rocks), and the identity mis-reads (heather, clover, grass, pearl).

How this was evaluated

Every tile was rendered with the actual tile-drawing code (esbuild-bundled straight from showcaseTiles.ts, so it can't drift from the board), composited onto the real board disc colour for its category, at the true −24..+24 design box inside the 74px tile. For each tile two artifacts were produced and read close-up: a contact sheet (4 season stills + an 8-frame sweep of each idle tier + 6-frame strips of all three transitions) and a high-res zoom pack. The two idle tiers were isolated by scanning the idle loop's motion-energy and picking the calm-beat and rare-action crests — the same technique the in-repo preview uses. 13 reviewers (one per family group) then cross-checked every render against the source module, several writing numerical probes to measure seam error, transition-endpoint error and idle-energy ratios rather than eyeball them.

Faithful, not the board. Renders use the same disc gradient, design box and draw code as paintTileCanvas — so contrast/readability calls reflect the real game. The one caveat: the "major idle" strip shows the highest-energy frame of the loop; for a tile that has no rare tier (fish, minerals) that frame is just a phase of the common loop, which is itself the finding.

To watch these animate, the live preview at reference/docs/seasonal-vector-tiles/index.html plays the full year (idle + morphs) per tile. This doc is the written assessment; that page is the motion reference.

Seasonal keyframes — are they good, or should we change them?

The bar: four seasons that read boldly different at a glance, while the subject identity stays locked (same plant/animal — only colour, volume and dressing change), and winter that is frosted, not whited-out. Most tiles clear this bar with room to spare. Three groups do not, and each has a clean, shared fix.

1 · Winter "white-out" — the #1 keyframe defect

The single most common keyframe problem: a winter preset that washes a full-tile frost rectangle and/or an oversized snow cap over the subject's lit face, desaturating it until identity is lost. The tiles that handle winter well do the opposite — they keep snow as a crest above the subject and dust frost as discrete specks, leaving the lit face saturated.

TileWhat winter does wrongSource
carrotFull-tile frost fillRect(-30,-30,60,60) @0.26 + snow cap over ~half the root; orange survives as a sliver, green top greys out.carrot.ts:590–632
broccoliFrost wash + cap + gloss smooth the buds into a glassy blue-grey dome — reads like a mushroom, not broccoli.broccoli.ts:553–586
squashFrost ellipse over the upper ~45% + cooled skin desaturate the golden gourd to pale cream above the waist.squash.ts:514–518
oakWinter branch silhouette over-frosts to a white feather/starburst; the deciduous tree shape is gone.oak.ts:231–256
birchSnow load on already near-white bark → another white blob.birch.ts:495–530
willowWinter drops leafDensity to 0.2 — the weeping curtain (its identity) disappears, leaving a post under a snow bar.willow.ts:233
pear / mannaMilder: pear leans grey-sage under heavy frost; manna's pale grains + snow nearly merge into one white shape.pear.ts SP.Winter · manna.ts
The rule to propagate (already proven by beet, turnip, mushroom, apple, lemon, coconut, palm): snow accumulates as a crest above / on top of the subject; frost is discrete specks, not a fill; the lit face keeps its season colour. Winter should say "cold," not "erased."

2 · Identity mis-reads — a few stills read as the wrong object

Independent of motion, a handful of stills don't read as their subject at board size. These are silhouette/occlusion problems, not animation problems.

  • heather → reads as a brown pot / cauldron (one hard foliage ellipse on a pedestal of twigs; flowers biased to the upper dome leave a bare "bowl wall"). Worst silhouette in the set. POLISH heather.ts:495–506
  • clover → reads as a green cake dome (filled radial-gradient body). clover.ts:633–645
  • grass → reads as an agave / seashell fan (a symmetric rigid blade fan). grass.ts:281
  • giant pearl → reads as a dark navy slab — but this one is a bug (occlusion), not a style call. See Fixes → P0.

3 · Mineral seasons are too flat

A rock genuinely doesn't change with the seasons the way a plant does, so "bold obvious seasons" is hardest here — and as shipped, the minerals don't clear it. For all seven ore tiles, Spring ≈ Summer ≈ Autumn; only Winter (the snow cap) reads. The design correctly refuses to re-hue the mineral (identity stays locked), but it then leans almost entirely on a global light tint that is far too weak (lightAmt ≤ 0.20) to register at board size.

This needs a rethink (in the presets, not the architecture). Levers that would make inanimate seasons read, in priority order: (1) wetness/sheen as the spring↔summer axis — damp, glossier, cool-rimmed rock in spring vs dry, warm, lower-contrast in summer (a real material change rocks undergo, currently absent); (2) moss as a feature, not a sub-pixel fleck — a lichen patch crawling up one face in spring that recedes by autumn; (3) oxidation for the metal ores (iron rust-bloom in damp spring, copper verdigris) — material-true seasonal colour the locked palette can still respect; (4) autumn leaves caught in the crevices at full size, not 3px specks. coal already does a partial version (its gloss/shadow swings with the season) and is the model to copy.

What's working — the keyframe exemplars

Worth stating plainly: most season sets are strong. The bar to copy: apple / lemon (bold ripeness arc, locked identity), rambutan / starfruit (hue-driven swings — the lesson is hue beats value: the tiles that swing hue read best), buckwheat (flower→seed-head swap + autumn leaves), turnip / beet (root-veg with snow placed above the leaves), water-lily (winter freezes the whole pool), and palm (frosted, never snowed-under).

Idle & the WC3 two-tier action

The intended idle is two-tier: a calm, frequent common beat (a peck, a sway, a graze) plus a rare, bold "WC3-style" action every ~12–18s (a hop, a crow, a head-butt, a flush). Both must be seamless — anim(t=0) equals the season still, and every gesture returns to rest with zero value and zero velocity at the window edges, so the loop never pops.

The machinery is excellent. Across birds, herd, cattle, mounts, fruit, grain and veg the reviewers measured anim(0) ≡ draw and transition(0)/transition(1) ≡ the season stills to 0 pixels. The raised-cosine windows and lerpP∘smoothstep transitions make seamlessness hold by construction. This is a real strength — keep it.

What "lively enough" looks like — the reference rig

The chicken/sheep family is the template, and it is genuinely fun. The common peck reads as pecking; the rare hop has anticipation (a crouch), an airborne stretch, and a squash landing — and every on-board instance fires it in lockstep, WC3-style.

Every character family has its own bespoke rare action, which is what keeps the board from feeling templated: rooster's vertical crow, pheasant's flush with a streaming tail, turkey's gobble + tail-fan, dodo's flap-and-fail with a dedicated plop, goat's full head-butt, mammoth's trunk-curl trumpet, pig-in-disguise's failed hop that slips its mask. This is the strongest, most distinctive animation work in the game.

The liveliness gap — 17 tiles have no rare tier at all

The two-tier idle was rolled out to land creatures and produce but never reached the water or the mine. All 8 fish and all 9 mineral/coin/special tiles animate with a single continuous loop — a gentle bob or a traveling glint — and define no t-gated rare action. Measured idle-energy "rare ÷ common" ratios sit at 0.94–1.10 for these tiles (a real rare action is well above ~1.5).

Fish (8/8 — no rare tier)

ink is the closest to a model: its rising ink-wisp could become a gated burst with one change. The rest (clam, oyster, mackerel, kelp, sardine, cocoa, jade) want a real occasional beat: a shell gape, a darting wriggle, an ink puff, a kelp gust. BUG cocoa flashes a glint on settle (cocoa.ts:551, sheen>=0 guard draws what the still lacks).

Minerals + coin + pearl (9/9 — no rare tier)

Every anim() is one continuous traveling glint whose duty cycle is so high the "calm" beat never rests. These tiles are ideal for a rare flash: a gem refraction starburst, a coal ember spark, a coin cha-ching glint, a pearl lustre pulse — every ~15s.

Where the rare tier exists but underwhelms

TileIssueMeasure / location
wild_goose REWORKRare honk/take-off is no bigger than the routine peck — effectively absent. Neck rises ~17px, body barely lifts.ratio 1.01 · wildGoose.ts:160
boar REWORKRare head-toss is real but swamped by an over-energetic common chew — damp the chew or boost the toss.ratio 1.17 · boar.ts:158/176
grass DESIGNTiers inverted: the rare critter-peek is quieter than the common wind gust — backwards from the WC3 ideal.grass.ts:536–583
turkey BUGRare gobble (phase 4.0) collides with the recurring t=15 peck; energy never returns to 0 between them, so the rare action loses its clean settle.turkey.ts:163
melon / pepper / cucumber ENHANCEWeakest produce rare beats; cucumber's straight-up hop is wrong for a lying-down subject (wants a base-pivot teeter).cucumber.ts:693–711

Consistency: produce shares one copy-paste bounce

All the veg and most of the fruit reuse a near-identical squash-stretch hop for the rare action. Each subject is distinct, but the motion is the same — so adjacent produce tiles read as copy-pasted. (The character families avoid this by giving each species its own action.)

Cross-cutting consistency findings

Issues that span families — each is a single pattern worth fixing once and propagating.

A · Ambient dressing breaks the loop seam in non-summer seasons

The pose returns to rest at t=0 (seamless), but the drifting ambient particles — falling petals, autumn leaves, settling snow, drifting chaff, phoenix embers, the fish water-shimmer — are phased on the raw clock t, independent of the pose window. So at t=0 they're mid-flight and pop on the loop wrap. Reviewers measured a seam error of ~350–2400 in non-summer seasons vs exactly 0 in summer (which has no dressing).

Not currently player-visible — the engine's idle clock never resets, so an on-board tile just keeps drifting. But it is a latent correctness issue and it is visible in any looped preview (and is the root of phoenix's and oak's seam reports). Cheap fix: phase ambient particles on the pose period, or gate them by a (1−cos) envelope so they begin and end at rest.

B · Winter dressing conventions diverge

Covered in depth under Keyframes: some tiles snow-cap above the subject (correct), others wash a frost rect over the lit face (white-out). Standardize on the crest-above + frost-specks convention.

C · The winter scarf reads as anatomy

Several animals share a red winter scarf that, placed high, reads as part of the creature — a bloody tongue/snarl on the dark boar, a wattle on the white sheep/alpaca. The two that look right (hog, ram) use a cool colour seated low on the neck base. Give each a distinct cool hue, low.

D · Pale & matching-disc subjects lose contrast

A recurring readability tax where the subject's value matches its board disc: goose / alpaca / goat / sheep (white-on-snow, measured "mark-on-snow" ~25–28, the danger zone), blackberry (dark-on-red, autumn collapses to a blob), gold coin & gold ore (face luminance ≈ the lit disc — saved only by the dark outline), silver (white veins on pale grey). Fix vocabulary: a cool rim-light / back-halo / firmer contact shadow on the at-risk winter and matching-disc presets.

E · Phase-scheduling smell

Many tiles fire their common and rare windows at overlapping phases (e.g. both near t≈3 on the cattle/mounts), and stagger the rare phase inconsistently across siblings. It's seam-safe today but it's what causes the turkey collision and makes an energy-based "find the rare action" detector mislabel frames. Adopt a consistent rare-phase offset that clears the common window.

F · Small shared props

Five trees (oak, birch, cypress, fir, willow) share an identical "robin" prop (same browns); only palm varies it. Minor, but it reads as a stamp when two trees sit together. Vary the bird (or drop it on some).

G · Transition technique: one outlier

Every tile tweens its transition by interpolating season parameters (lerpP) — exact endpoints, clean mid-morph — except oak, which crossfades two full translucent draws and therefore ghosts mid-transition. Port oak to the parameter-lerp pattern.

Per-tile scores (all 80)

exemplary ≥ 4.5 strong 4.0–4.4 polish 3.0–3.9 rework ≤ 2.5

Scores are 1–5. KF = season keyframes · IdC = common idle · WC3 = rare/major idle · Tr = transitions · Cn = consistency. Family = the board biome the tile appears in (a few subjects are authored in another folder).

Filter:

coconutfruit545554.5Excellent; tuft sway not window-gated (sub-pixel anim(0)≠still — trivial).

pepperveg443544.0Bold; rare hop reads "candy-apple on a stick" (weakest veg major).

broccoliveg344443.0Winter frost-wash → glassy dome reads like a mushroom cap.
carrotveg344443.0Winter near white-out (full-tile frost + huge cap); worst winter in veg.

water_lilyflower555545.0Best seasonal idea — winter freezes the pool + swaps the dragonfly for an ice-skitter.
pansyflower545555.0Clean bold violet→mauve→grey swing + a spring bud.
spikygrass545555.0Proves negative-space ground cover reads right; great gust-bend with overshoot.
meadowgrass444444.0Solid ground cover.
grassgrass342433.0Reads as an agave fan; idle tiers INVERTED (rare critter quieter than common gust).
heathergrass344422.5Reads as a pot/cauldron (worst silhouette in the set); motion itself is fine.
chickenbird555555.0The reference rig; clean on every axis.
pheasantbird555555.0Best identity + most cinematic rare burst (crouch→flush, tail streaming).
roosterbird555555.0Vertical CROW rare action; great.
henbird555555.0Hop+flap; clean.
parrotbird554555.0Most vivid stills; rich common idle.
phoenixbird545545.0Most ambitious — coherent fire-in-winter; ambient embers break a perfect loop (not player-visible).
dodobird455555.0Flap-and-fail hop with a dedicated PLOP landing.
pig_in_disguisebird545555.0The gag reads; rare failed-hop slips the mask — best theme↔mechanic fusion.
turkeybird545544.0BUG: rare gobble (phase 4.0) collides with the t=15 peck — never settles (turkey.ts:163).
goosebird454554.0All-white → winter whiteout risk on snow (no coloured head part); saved by outline + bill.
melonbird443454.0Warm-season greens compress (spring≈summer≈autumn); weakest produce rare beat.
wild_goosebird442543.0Rare action effectively absent (energy ratio 1.01) — needs a real lift + honk.
cloverbird344433.0Reads as a green cake dome (filled body) — open up the silhouette.

warthogherd445544.5Distinct (tall sparse spikes, big tusks); strong rare action.

kelpfish442543.5No rare tier; gentle sway only — wants a current gust.
sardinefish442553.5Best fish consistency; same swim-flex t=0 ease-in; no rare tier.

oysterfish441543.0No rare tier; a pearl-flash gape would carry it.
jadefish441543.0No rare tier.

coalmine2.53.5144.53.5EXEMPLARY craft — faceted, glossy-not-a-hole; only rock whose season is partly subject-carried.
coin_goldencoin2.541.5443.5Most characterful subject; gold-on-gold (face lum ≈ disc) saved only by the outline.

Per-family long-form findings (verdict, cross-cutting notes, keyframe recommendations) were written per batch during the review; the rows above distill them.

Fixes & recommendations

Ordered by priority. P0 are correctness bugs (something renders wrong or pops); P1 are the high-impact polish themes; P2 are enhancements that raise the ceiling.

P0 · Correctness bugs (locatable)

#TileBugLocationFix direction
1giant_pearlFront shell valve is drawn over the pearl and paints it dark navy (lum ~80 vs intended ~233). Does not read as a pearl.giantPearl.ts:491–505Clip the front valve to y > pearlBottom, or draw the pearl after the front valve.
2longhornRare head-swing slews the head ±17px sideways with no drawn neck → the head detaches from the barrel.longhorn.ts:151Add a tracking neck wedge (mirror horse) and/or cut the swing to ±6–8px.
3turkeyRare gobble window (phase 4.0) abuts the t=15 peck; energy never returns to 0 between them — the rare action loses its settle.turkey.ts:163Offset the rare phase (4.0 → 6.0) to clear the common window.
4oak(a) Autumn anim(0)≠draw — the still freezes leaf-fall at 0.25 but anim starts at 0 → leaves jump on entry. (b) Only tile using crossfade transitions → mid-morph ghosting.oak.ts:692–695, 742–871Start ambient fall at the still's value (or gate it); port transitions to lerpP.
5cocoaIdle draws a bright rib-glint that the still lacks (if (sheen >= 0) fires at sheen=0) → a flash on every season settle.cocoa.ts:551Change the guard to > 0 or gate the glint by a window envelope.
6coconutMinor: tuft sway is a constant offset, not window-gated → anim(0) differs from the still by a sub-pixel ~0.38px.coconut.ts:727Envelope the sway term so it's 0 at t=0.

P1 · High-impact polish themes

Fix the winter white-outs

Re-dress winter on carrot, broccoli, squash, pear, oak, birch, willow, manna: snow as a crest above, frost as specks, lit face stays saturated. Copy turnip/beet/palm. 8 tiles

Fix the identity mis-reads

heather (pot→shrub), clover (cake→leaves), grass (fan→tufts): open the silhouette with negative space, as spiky/water-lily already do. 3 tiles

Lift the matching-disc contrast

Cool rim-light / back-halo on goose, alpaca, sheep, goat (white-on-snow), blackberry (dark-on-red), gold/coin/silver (metal-on-metal). contrast

Seam the ambient dressing

Phase falling petals/leaves/snow/embers/shimmer on the pose period (or gate by (1−cos)) so they begin/end at rest. One shared helper fix. whole set

Cool, low winter scarves

The shared red scarf reads as a tongue/wattle. Move it to the neck base and recolour cool per animal (hog/ram already correct). herd

Strengthen weak rare actions

wild_goose (add lift + honk), boar (damp the chew / boost the toss), grass (un-invert the tiers). Target rare÷common ≥ 1.8. 3 tiles

P2 · Enhancements that raise the ceiling

  • Give fish & minerals a real two-tier idle (17 tiles). This is the biggest single lift for "liveliness": a shell gape, a darting wriggle, an ink-puff (ink already has the wisp), a gem refraction-starburst, a coin cha-ching glint, a pearl lustre pulse — one bold beat every ~12–18s. Also lower the minerals' continuous-glint duty cycle so the calm beat genuinely rests.
  • Rethink mineral seasons (7 rocks) via wetness/sheen + moss-as-feature + ore oxidation, per Keyframes §3. Architecture is fine; this is preset work.
  • Diversify the produce rare action — break the single copy-paste bounce into a few archetypes so a screen of crops doesn't pulse in unison.
  • Push the soft seasonal swings — golden_apple and melon lean on value where they should swing hue (the lesson rambutan/starfruit prove).
  • Vary the shared tree "robin" prop across oak/birch/cypress/fir/willow.
Bottom line. The vector seasonal-tile system is in good shape and its character animation is a genuine strength — keep the architecture and the chicken/sheep/mount rigs as the bar. The work that remains is well-defined and biome-targeted: squash the 6 P0 bugs, re-dress the winter white-outs, and bring fish + minerals up to the two-tier idle standard the rest of the board already meets.

Reference exemplars (copy these)

Animation rig

chicken / sheep (the two-tier template), mammoth (safest anticipation gating), pheasant / goat (most cinematic rare actions).

Season keyframes

apple / lemon (ripeness arc), rambutan / starfruit (hue-driven), turnip / beet (winter done right), water-lily (whole-scene season).

Hard-case craft

palm (frosted-not-snowed), coal (glossy dark subject on a dark disc; subject-carried season), eggplant (saturated subject that never goes muddy).
